summary: Jeff Atwood argues against solo software development, contending that while programmers may be drawn to solitary work with computers, working alone on software projects is ultimately counterproductive. He cites a cautionary tale about the dangers of being the sole developer: no code reviews, no process enforcement, no one to catch mistakes or share the workload. Despite the allure of complete control, solo development is 'debilitating' rather than empowering. Atwood reflects on his own introversion and early computing experiences, but concludes that modern software development is inherently collaborative, and programmers should actively seek out peers to learn from and work alongside.
key_points: Solo development eliminates accountability structures like code review, coding standards enforcement, and estimate verification
Working alone offers the illusion of control but actually leaves developers more vulnerable and less capable
The internet has transformed programming from a solitary activity into one that requires constant connection to collective knowledge
Programmers should deliberately seek out smart colleagues and aim to be 'the dumbest guy in the room' to accelerate learning
Software development is far more social than the stereotypical introvert programmer realizes
money_quotes: There is no process in a team of one.
In a vacuum, no one can hear you scream.

Working alone means complete control over a software project, wielding ultimate power over every decision. But working on a software project all by yourself, instead of being empowering, is paradoxically debilitating.
It's a shifting mirage that offers the tantalizing promise of relief, while somehow leaving you thirstier and weaker than you started.
No one ever said on their deathbed, 'Gee, I wish I had spent more time alone with my computer.'
What good are nifty coding tricks if you can't show them off to anyone?
If you're serious about programming, you should demand to work with your peers.
Endeavor to be the dumbest guy in the room, and you'll quickly discover that software development is a far more social activity than most people realize.
key_insight: The freedom of solo development is a trap—real growth and sustainable software work require the accountability, mentorship, and collaboration that only come from working with other programmers.
